planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in early to mid-spring in consistently moist to wet, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; ostrich fern thrives in riparian and floodplain conditions and spreads vigorously by stolons, so allow ample space for a colony to develop.

directions: Matteuccia struthiopteris is a large, dramatic, deciduous native fern of eastern and northern North America, producing tall, vase-shaped clusters of bright-green sterile fronds that resemble the plumes of an ostrich and reach 3–5 feet tall; shorter, dark-brown fertile fronds emerge in the center of the vase in late summer and persist through winter, releasing spores in early spring. It spreads assertively by underground stolons and will form a bold, large-scale colony in moist, cool, shaded sites, making it ideal for naturalizing along stream banks, pond edges, and shaded rain gardens in zones 3–7, though it struggles in the heat and humidity of the Deep South. The tightly coiled spring fiddleheads are a celebrated edible delicacy — harvest only a few per plant when they are still tightly coiled and no more than a few inches tall, and cook thoroughly before eating as raw fiddleheads contain a heat-labile toxin. Pair with native wildflowers such as Virginia bluebells, Jack-in-the-pulpit, and trout lily for a classic northeastern floodplain garden.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in wet to consistently moist, peaty, acidic soil in partial to full shade; netted chain fern is well adapted to boggy woodland margins and shaded swale edges where soils remain saturated for extended periods.

directions: Woodwardia areolata is a small, deciduous native fern of eastern North American bogs, swamps, and shaded wet woodland margins, producing simply pinnate (not bipinnate) sterile fronds with broad, lobed, ladder-like pinnae and a distinctive netted venation pattern that gives the plant its common name; its fertile fronds are narrower with elongated sori arranged in chain-like rows along the midrib of each pinna. It spreads steadily by slender rhizomes to form a low, dense groundcover in wet, acidic, shaded sites and is an excellent native choice for rain garden margins, shaded bog edges, and the wet fringes of pond plantings in zones 3–9. Unlike many ferns, it tolerates standing water and waterlogged soils well, making it one of the few ferns suited to intermittently flooded conditions. Combine with blue flag iris, pitcher plants, and native sedges in naturalized wet woodland or bog plantings.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; New York fern spreads readily by rhizomes and will naturalize to form a weed-suppressing groundcover in moist, shaded woodland settings.

directions: Thelypteris noveboracensis is a light-green, deciduous native fern of eastern North American moist woodlands, instantly recognizable by its distinctive frond shape — the pinnae taper gradually toward both the tip and the base of the frond, creating a narrowed appearance at both ends that has inspired the saying that it "burns at both ends." It spreads steadily by slender, branching rhizomes to form a delicate, airy groundcover that is excellent for naturalizing large shaded areas under deciduous trees, and its light, fresh green color brightens deeply shaded spots in spring and early summer before the fronds fade to yellow and go dormant by fall. New York fern prefers moderately moist, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic woodland soils and does not tolerate waterlogging or drought, though it is more adaptable than many woodland ferns once established. Pair with wild ginger, mayapple, and Solomon's seal for a classic eastern woodland groundcover planting.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; northern lady fern is adaptable to a range of moisture levels once established and makes a graceful, easy-care addition to shaded borders and woodland gardens throughout northern and mountain regions.

directions: Athyrium angustum (often treated as Athyrium filix-femina var. angustum, the northern lady fern) is a graceful, deciduous native fern of eastern and central North America, producing broadly triangular to lanceolate, bipinnate-pinnatifid fronds with a finely textured, airy appearance on arching, pale-green to reddish-tinged stipes; it is extremely similar to the widely grown common lady fern (A. filix-femina) and shares its elegant, vase-shaped habit. Northern lady fern prefers consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade and tolerates wet conditions well, though it will also persist in moderately dry shade once established, making it more adaptable than many woodland ferns. It is an excellent, trouble-free native substitute for the popular Japanese painted fern (A. niponicum) and its cultivars, and combines beautifully with hostas, astilbe, native bleeding heart, and trilliums. Divide clumps every few years in spring to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in moist, rocky, humus-rich, circumneutral to slightly alkaline soil in partial to full shade; bulblet bladder fern naturally colonizes mossy rocks and cliff faces and benefits from a gritty, well-drained substrate even while needing consistent moisture.

directions: Cystopteris bulbifera is a delicate, deciduous native fern of eastern North American rocky, moist, shaded woodlands and cliff faces, producing finely dissected, lacy, narrowly triangular fronds on long, pale-pink to rosy stipes that arch gracefully and often drape over rocky outcrops; its most distinctive feature is the small, pea-sized bulblets that form on the undersides of fronds, especially near the midrib, which drop to the ground and develop into new plants, giving the colony a gently self-spreading character over time. It prefers moist, humus-rich, rocky soils with good drainage and a circumneutral to slightly alkaline pH — often found on limestone cliffs and talus slopes — and is an excellent choice for shaded rock gardens, crevice walls, and naturalizing over boulders in zones 3–8. Its extraordinarily delicate, lacy texture contrasts beautifully with bold-leafed shade plants such as hostas and large-leafed trilliums. In warm weather the fronds may go summer-dormant if conditions become dry; consistent moisture extends the growing season.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; marginal wood fern is one of the most drought- and dry-shade-tolerant native ferns and is an excellent choice for shaded slopes, rocky woodland gardens, and the dry root zones beneath large trees.

directions: Dryopteris marginalis is a handsome, semi-evergreen to evergreen native fern of eastern North American rocky woodlands, prized for its leathery, blue-green fronds that emerge in a graceful vase shape in spring and persist through winter in most of its range, providing exceptional year-round structure in shaded gardens; the sori are arranged in rows along the margins of the pinnules — hence the common name marginal wood fern, also called marginal shield fern. It is one of the easiest and most adaptable native ferns, tolerating drier shade than most Dryopteris species and thriving in well-drained, rocky, or even somewhat gravelly soils under deciduous trees where other ferns decline; it is far more drought-resilient than interrupted fern or cinnamon fern. The evergreen fronds hold their blue-green color through winter, making it an outstanding companion for other winter-interest shade plants such as Christmas fern, hellebores, and native gingers. Divide established clumps in early spring every 4–5 years to maintain vigor and rejuvenate the crown.
